text: cannot be posited as a ‘first’ or ‘elementary’ building block, onto which one can erect an entire edifice. These are the fantasies of a desk-scholar. As van Gennep would stress in other contexts, the aborigines (here, in particular, the Arunta) actually have rather complex matrimonial rules and rather complex totemic beliefs and practices. Even if one wants to defend an evolutionist perspective, these people simply cannot be taken to represent some kind of ursprung. It is the very foundation of Durkheim’s entire argument that is thoroughly unsound. Van Gennep also denudes Durkheim in another way, returning to one of the points raised in his 1906 discussion. Durkheim had programmatically stated his wish to liberate social explanations from historical ones. And yet, when Durkheim lists the various constitutive parts of a religious system (such as the variety of rites), instead of actually understanding them or their ‘function’, he uses them to construct, again and again, a speculative theory about their ‘origins’ (ibid.: 94). Furthermore, and just as seriously, in his insistence on staking all on the collective level, Durkheim completely and categorically neglects the action of Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 55 single, concrete individuals in the formation of institutions and beliefs. In doing so, the very process and production that lies behind myth-telling and ritual acting is annulled and hidden away. 7 It was this process formation that van Gennep had in fact tried to discuss in his own 1906 book, Mythes et Légendes – a book, says van Gennep, that Durkheim has ‘conscienscieusement neglected’ (my italics; one notes here van Gennep’s deep bitterness but also utter surprise; and he is, of course, targeting Mauss just as well, for we know from the letters from Durkheim to Mauss that it was the latter who did most of the literature reviewing). Even in the most ‘primitive’ societies, van Gennep insists, individuals do act. Durkheim dreams of assigning society a natural reality with its own laws of necessity, in a world deprived of concrete human beings. For the purposes of his theoretical construct, van Gennep continues, Durkheim has artificially reduced Australian society to a ‘monocellular organism’, devoid of agency. Durkheim possesses nothing but a metaphysic and scholastic understanding of the world; he constructs reality from pre-conceived words and concepts (ibid.). And then follows what is perhaps the most emblematic statement ever made about Durkheim and his entire sociology: Not having the sense of life, that is to say the biological and ethnographic sense, he turns phenomena and living beings into scientifically dissected plants, as in an herbarium. (ibid.: 94) It is not simply the data that is wrong; it is not the flawed methodology; it is not even the circular, redundant nature of Durkheim’s theory that is the issue: the problem is that Durkheim lacks a sense of life. This statement boils down the fundamental problem that van Gennep had identified before anyone else: Durkheim’s project did not even belong to the life sciences. It was lifeless and futile at the best; theoretically misleading and politically dangerous at the worst. 7 In fact, the telling of myths is probably one of those activities that actually require individual originality and impetus. Myths, like songs or poems and most art-forms, simply cannot be produced by collectivities. They are reproduced by collectivities – but that is a different matter. Here we must return to Weber’s premise, stated in the Introduction to The Social Psychology of the World Religions. Here Weber starts from the empirical fact that human beings are differently qualified in a religious way, and says that this fact ‘stands at the beginning of the history of religion’ (Weber 1948: 287). Weber claims that certain ecstatic and visionary capacities, a ‘charisma’, might be awakened in some but not in all (it is in this context that Weber talks about the masses – and he clearly seems to include himself – being religiously ‘unmusical’). In other words, Weber takes status stratification for granted at the basis of both political and religious movements: there is ‘heroic’ or ‘virtuoso’ religiosity as opposed to mass religiosity. Van Gennep always had a keen eye for these given differentials; in one of his lesser known essays, he discusses why some people just happen to have a good sense of direction, counting himself among the lucky ones. Van Gennep was himself a genius with languages, but he was not good at mathematics, no matter how hard he tried. Liminality and the Modern 56 It was this same recognition that 50 years later would compel Victor Turner to look for alternative frameworks which would allow him to start over again, addressing social life and its drama. The conclusion is that ‘M. Durkheim’, in his eagerness to explain religion, simply has replaced it with his own prefabricated ‘system’, his inherently impossible sociological imperative. And it is this entire enterprise that van Gennep declares useless and void. Radcliffe-Brown’s Critique There is no doubt that van Gennep’s discussion of Durkheim is the sharpest and most to the point critique ever formulated. He continued his discussion of Durkheim in a series of articles on totemism, and some of these laid the framework for the book on totemism, L’état actuel du problème totémique, van Gennep’s main thesis for his doctorate of letters, published in 1920. 8 After that, and coinciding with his abandonment of sociology and anthropology, van Gennep mostly gave up on the fight. It was a lost cause; all he could do was to follow through with his own principles within folklore. 9 While van Gennep’s review stands out for its sharpness and frank use of speech, it is worth remembering that he was not completely alone in this critique. In August 1912 Radcliffe-Brown (who also knew the ethnographic material rather well) wrote a letter to Mauss in which he expressed his disappointment with Durkheim’s superficial and erroneous treatment of the ethnographic data.
